flags: DEFECT: step 3 (original index 2) calls for a 'center 2x4 rail' but there is no 2x4 in materials[] and no center-rail row in cutList. The rewrite names the gap in the prose rather than inventing a part.
DEFECT: the original last step says the center leg supports are 'called out in the cut list'. They are not in the cut list. Rewritten to say so.

DERIVED (and proved by the plan's own note): the top/bottom rails run the FULL outside width and the side rails fit between them. The note gives 57/63/79/75 for full/queen/king/cal-king, which is mattress width + 3 (two 1-1/2" rail thicknesses) in every case; the 38" slats then span exactly the 41" twin rail less 3".

DERIVED: cleat position (flush with the rail's bottom edge) is not stated in the source. It is offered with the arithmetic (2-1/4" slat height, 3-1/4" of rail proud) so a builder can choose otherwise.
DERIVED: slat spacing (~4-7/16") is arithmetic on ten 3-1/2" slats over the 75" span, not an authored value.

step.title: Cut the frame parts
step.body: Cut for the size you are actually building; the numbers in the cut list are the twin. For a twin, cut two side rails 75" and two top/bottom rails 41" from the 2x6, two cleats 75" from the 2x2, ten slats 38" from the 1x4, and — if you want the optional bracing — four corner braces 8" from 2x6 with both ends at 45°. The top/bottom rail length is your mattress width plus 3", which is why the note reads 57", 63", 79" and 75" for full, queen, king and cal-king: those two rails run the full outside width of the box and the side rails sit between them. Three 8 ft 2x6s cover all the 2x6 work — a 75" side rail plus two 8" braces on each of two boards, and both 41" rails on the third. Each 8 ft 2x2 gives one 75" cleat, and each 8 ft 1x4 gives two 38" slats, so the five listed 1x4s yield exactly ten.

ops.body: Lay each 75" side rail inside-face up and run a 75" 2x2 cleat along it, flush with the rail's bottom edge and flush at both ends. Drive 2-1/2" screws through the cleat into the rail every 10" to 12", staggering them so the 2x2 does not split along one grain line. With the cleat at the bottom, the 3/4" slats end up 2-1/4" above the bottom of the 5-1/2" rail and the 2x6 still stands about 3-1/4" proud of the slat surface, which is what keeps the mattress from sliding off the sides. Do both rails identically, and be sure the cleats end up on the faces that will be inside the box.

Stand the two 41" top/bottom rails on edge and set the 75" side rails between them, cleats facing inward and all top edges flush. Drive three 2-1/2" screws through the face of each end rail into the end grain of each side rail, pre-drilling so the 2x6 does not split. That gives a box 41" x 78" outside with a 38" x 75" opening — exactly a twin mattress — so measure both diagonals and rack the frame until they match before the last screws go home.

For full size and up, run a center rail down the length of the box between the two end rails — the same clear span as the side rails, 75" on a twin-length frame — and set it so its top face is flush with the tops of the 2x2 cleats, because the slats have to bear on the cleats and the center rail at the same time. Screw it to the end rails with 2-1/2" screws driven through the outside faces. Be aware that the materials list and cut list contain no 2x4 line for this part, so add one board to your shopping for anything larger than a twin.

Cut the four 8" corner braces so the long edge measures 8" and the two 45° ends run opposite ways, which lets each brace sit flat across an inside corner with both ends bearing on the rail faces. Tuck each brace down against the cleats so it cannot foul the slats, then drive two 2-1/2" screws through the side rail and two through the end rail into it. They are optional, but on a 78"-long box they are what stops the frame racking when someone sits down on a corner.

Lay the ten 38" slats across the cleats with one slat tight against each end rail and the remaining eight spread evenly between. Ten 3-1/2" slats use 35" of the 75" span, leaving 40" of gap across nine spaces — about 4-7/16" between slats. Drive one 1-1/2" to 2" screw through each slat end into the cleat below; one screw per end is enough and it lets a slat be lifted out later without splitting it. Do not glue them.

Turn the frame over and mount a leg at each corner, set in far enough that the mounting plate lands fully on the 1-1/2" thickness of both rails rather than overhanging the outside face. Use the screws that came with the legs — a 2-1/2" screw driven through a 1-1/2" rail comes out the other side. Turn it back over and check it does not rock before the mattress goes on.

A king or cal-king frame is 79" or 75" across, and at that span the center rail needs its own path to the floor rather than hanging off the end rails. Add one leg under the middle of the center rail, or two spaced evenly along it, using the same leg hardware as the corners. Note that the cut list does not actually contain the center leg parts this step refers to, so budget an extra leg set for a king-size build.

baseStepTitles: Attach the cleats to the side rails
Add center leg support for king sizes

flags: SHORTFALL (3/4" plywood): the two listed 4x8 sheets cannot yield the cut list. Each 48" x 59-1/2" side panel must come out of its own sheet (59-1/2" exceeds the 48" sheet width), leaving 48" x 36-1/2" on each, i.e. 3,504 sq in. The two 36" x 31-3/4" roof panels, the 36" x 16" roof panel, the 7-5/8" x 36" small wall, the 12" x 32-1/2" nesting-box door and the two 16-1/4" x 23-3/4" clean-out doors total 4,298 sq in — before any base-door skin. A third sheet is needed. Area comparison is nesting-independent.

SHORTFALL (cedar 2x4): the two 10 ft cedar/treated 2x4s are fully consumed by the four base 2x4s (58-1/2" + 59-1/2" = 118" per 120" board), leaving no cedar for the 33-1/2" door divider that step 2 assigns to that material. Six of the seven plain 8 ft 2x4s cover every inside-coop framing part, so the spare plain board is the honest source.

The 'Planter frame 2x2s' cut-list row declares length 56-1/2" but its note names two different lengths (49-1/2" and 56-1/2") and says the planter bottom comes from scrap. Both lengths are stated in the rewrite; the planter's finished dimensions are not determinable.
Base door skin sizes are not in the cut list ('Clean-out doors' carries a 'measure and cut to fit' note); the rewrite does not assert them.

step.title: Cut the framing (and lay out the plywood before cutting it)
step.body:
Cut all the framing first and hold the plywood until both side-panel profiles are drawn on the sheets. From the cedar or treated stock: four legs 33-1/2" from the 4x4s; two base 2x8s 58-1/2" and two 59-1/2"; two base 2x4s 58-1/2" and two 59-1/2"; the center 2x8 at 56-1/2"; the 33-1/2" door divider; and eight base door frame pieces at 22". From the plain 2x4s: five floor supports at 33", coop framing two at 33", three at 30" and two at 43-1/2", and four roof supports at 29-5/8" measured long point to long point with the 45° ends NOT parallel to each other. Cut the 1x3 tray sides two at 46-1/2" and the ends two at 32-1/2", and the 2x2 planter cleat and rim at 49-1/2" and 56-1/2".

Two yield facts worth knowing before you shop. The two 10 ft cedar 2x4s are used up by the base alone (58-1/2" plus 59-1/2" is 118" on a 120" board), so take the 33-1/2" door divider from the plain 2x4s — six of the seven cover every inside-coop part and the seventh is spare. And plan on a third sheet of 3/4" plywood: each 48" x 59-1/2" side panel consumes one of the two listed sheets down to a 48" x 36-1/2" remainder, and the roof panels, small wall, nesting-box door and clean-out doors need more area than those remainders hold.

ops.body: Stand the two 46-1/2" 1x3 sides on edge and cap them with the 32-1/2" ends running the full width across their ends — that is the reading the parts themselves prove, because 46-1/2" plus 3/4" of end stock at each end is exactly the 48" length of the 1/2" plywood floor. Screw the 32-1/2" x 48" floor to the bottom edges with 2" screws, giving a tray 32-1/2" wide, 48" long and 2-1/2" deep. Sand the outside corners and edges so nothing hangs up, then check it slides freely through the gap you left between two floor supports before the coop is finished around it. Bed it with shavings and enjoy the best feature of the whole build every single week: pull, dump, hose, return.

Screw the five floor supports across the base with 3" screws — spaced to leave the clean-out tray's slot between two of them (check your tray width against the gap now, not when you fit the clean-out tray).

Lay out both side-panel profiles on the 3/4" sheets, nesting the shapes so the roof panels come out of the remainder, and cut with the jigsaw. Attach the panels to the floor supports.

baseStepTitles: Floor supports and shaped side panels

flags: SHORTFALL (Pine 1x3): materials list two 8 ft 1x3s. The four leg parts (2 @ 33-1/2", 2 @ 16-3/4") consume both. The fifteen 39" slats fit two per 8 ft board, so they need eight more boards — roughly seven beyond what is listed. Verified with packBoards at 1/8" kerf: legs+slats together need 9 boards of 96".

GAP: the tools list contains no crosscut saw — only a jigsaw, noted for the headboard curve. Every straight cut in this plan has no listed tool. The inserted cut step is tagged with the jigsaw because it is the only saw available; a miter or circular saw is what the work actually wants.

DERIVED and self-consistent: the side rails attach inside the 40-1/2" headboard/footboard width, giving a 39" clear opening that exactly matches the 39" slat length (40-1/2 less 3/4 of rail thickness at each end).
DERIVED: slat spacing. Fifteen 2-1/2" slats over a 75" rail leave 37-1/2" across fourteen gaps, about 2-11/16" — a little wider than the source's rough 'about 2"'.
DERIVED: the top trim count (8) over four posts is two per post; the source does not say how they land.
Finish is never mentioned in materials[] and the plan has no finish step.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body:
Cut two headboard rails 40-1/2" from the 1x8 — both come off the one 8 ft board — the footboard panel 40-1/2" from the 4 ft 1x10, and two side rails 75" from the 1x6, one per 8 ft board. From 1x3 cut two headboard front legs 33-1/2" and two footboard front legs 16-3/4". From 1x2 cut two headboard side legs 33-1/2", two footboard side legs 16-3/4", eight top trim pieces 2-1/4" and two cleats 75"; that is everything three 8 ft 1x2s will yield, with roughly an inch to spare, so cut the two 75" cleats first. Leave the curve alone for now — cut the headboard rail square to 40-1/2" and shape it in the next step.

The fifteen 39" slats are not covered by the listed stock. Two 39" slats fit an 8 ft 1x3, so the slats alone want eight boards on top of the two the legs consume, against the two 1x3s in the materials list — buy about seven more if you are building without a box spring.

ops.body: The 1x2 side legs are the same 33-1/2" as the front legs, and each lands on the outside edge of a front leg — filling the 3/4" you held the rails back by and covering the rail ends. Glue and drive 2" finish nails through the 1x2 into the edge of the 1x3, keeping both tops flush. Built this way each post reads 2-1/2" across the front and 2-1/4" deep on the side, and no end grain shows on the outside of the bed.

There are eight 2-1/4" trim pieces and four posts, so each post top takes two — one on the front face and one on the side, meeting at the corner. Nail each piece on separately with 2" finish nails and glue rather than building a cap and dropping it over; the four posts will not be identical to the thousandth, and fitting piece by piece hides that. Keep every trim piece flush with the post top or very slightly proud, never below it.

Build the footboard exactly like the headboard, using the single 40-1/2" 1x10 panel in place of the two 1x8 rails and the shorter 16-3/4" legs. Hold the panel back 3/4" from the outside edge of each 1x3 front leg, same as the headboard, so the 1x2 side legs land in the same relationship. There is no curve at this end — the top edge stays straight, which is the only difference between the two ends of the bed.

Screw a 75" 1x2 cleat along the inside face of each 75" side rail with 1-1/4" screws and glue, a screw every 10" or so. Put the cleat at the bottom edge of the rail if a box spring is going in, or 1-1/2" down from the top edge if you are running slats — decide before you drill, because it sets the finished mattress height. Attach the rails to the inside faces of the headboard and footboard legs so the clear opening between the two rails comes out at 39", which is exactly the slat length; check it with a tape before the screws go home. Metal bed-rail brackets in place of screws let the whole bed knock down for moving.

If you are skipping the box spring, lay the fifteen 39" slats across the cleats with one at each end and the rest spread evenly along the 75" rails. Fifteen 2-1/2" slats occupy 37-1/2", which leaves 37-1/2" of gap across fourteen spaces — about 2-11/16" each, a shade wider than the source's rough 2". Screw each slat down with one 1-1/4" screw per end and no glue, so a cracked slat can be swapped out later.

baseStepTitles: Attach the side rails and cleats

flags: DEFECT: the face frame does not close on the carcass. Bottom rail 2-1/2" + stiles 43-1/4" + top rail 3-1/2" stacks to 49-1/4", against 48" cabinet sides — a 1-1/4" overrun. The source does not say which member is trimmed, so the rewrite tells the builder to fit the stiles to the actual box rather than picking a side.
GAP: the five shelves' vertical spacing is not specified anywhere in the plan. The rewrite fixes only the top and bottom (flush with the side ends) and leaves the middle three to the builder.
The 'Base feet' row is 2 pieces at 5-1/2" square for a 16-1/2"-wide cabinet; how they are placed is not stated.

The decorative handle is cut from 1x4 scrap and has no cut-list row; the 1x4 is consumed nearly exactly by the verticals, shelves and face-frame top rail (3 boards, ~7-7/8" total slack), so the handle has to come from the offcuts.
Both 8 ft 1x2s are consumed with about 1-1/2" of total slack — no room for a mis-cut.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 1x12s cut two cabinet sides 48", five shelves 15", the 43" door and an 11" blank for the gauge disc. The three 1x12 boards break down cleanly: one 8-footer gives a 48" side plus three 15" shelves, the other gives the second 48" side plus the 43" door, and the 4 ft board gives the last two shelves and the 11" disc blank. Cut the 1/4" plywood back 16-1/2" x 48"; from the 10 ft 1x3 cut the 16-1/2" bottom rail and two 43-1/4" stiles; from 1x4 cut the 16-1/2" top rail, four bookshelf verticals 44" and six bookshelf shelves 9-3/4". From 1x2 cut ten shelf trims 9-3/4", two short verticals 11-1/4", two short horizontals 4-1/4" and the 18" top strip. Both 1x2s and all three 1x4s are consumed almost exactly, so cut the longest pieces first and keep the offcuts — the pump handle has no cut-list row and has to come out of 1x4 scrap.

ops.body: The two 48" sides run the full height and the five 15" shelves fit between them — that is what makes the 16-1/2" x 48" plywood back cover the carcass exactly, since 15" plus 3/4" of side stock at each edge is 16-1/2". Set the top and bottom shelves flush with the ends of the sides; the plan does not fix the three middle shelf heights, so space them for what you intend to store. Join every shelf with pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws or with glue and brads — both hold here, because the back and the face frame lock the box in the next steps. Square the carcass on the diagonals, then glue and nail the back on, hitting every shelf edge as you go; that back is what keeps it square permanently.

Assemble the face frame flat on the bench with the 16-1/2" rails running the full width and the 43-1/4" stiles fitted between them, so the frame's width matches the carcass exactly. Check the arithmetic before you glue: 2-1/2" plus 43-1/4" plus 3-1/2" is 49-1/4" against a 48" cabinet side, so something has to give — the plan does not say what, so dry-fit against your actual box and trim the stiles to suit. Clamp it square, then glue it to the front of the carcass with 1-1/4" brads, outside edges flush. Measure the opening the finished frame leaves before you cut the door to final size.

Each tower is two 44" 1x4 verticals with three 9-3/4" 1x4 shelves fitted between them, which makes the tower 11-1/4" across the front — the same as the cabinet's 11-1/4" depth, so all three units line up front to back. Build both towers identically and flat on the bench, glue and brads at each shelf. The five 9-3/4" 1x2 trims per tower run across the front shelf edges and stiffen the shallow shelves as much as they dress them. Pocket holes are worth the trouble on the top and bottom joints, where a tall narrow tower takes the most racking.

Draw an 11" circle on the 11" blank, cut it with the jigsaw staying just outside the line, then sand back to the line — this is the one detail that reads 'gas pump' from across the room, and a lumpy circle shows from there too. Mount it centered on the upper cabinet face. Screw the two 5-1/2" square base feet under the cabinet, set back from the front edge so they do not show. The door is a single 43" length of 1x12: hang it on its hinges first, check the reveal all the way round, and only then fit the magnetic clasp.

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frame
Attach the side bookshelves and trim
Add the gauge, base feet, and door
Add the hose, handle, and finish details

flags: SHORTFALL (Pine 2x2): the twelve 2x2 parts (4 @ 12-3/4", 8 @ 32") need four 8 ft boards at 1/8" kerf; three are listed. Verified with packBoards.
SHORTFALL (Pine 1x3): the twenty-eight 2-1/2"-wide parts (12 @ 16", 12 @ 29-1/2", 4 @ 12-3/4") need seven 8 ft boards; six are listed. Verified with packBoards.

DEFECT: the drawer boxes are 16" deep but the end panels and slide cleats are only 12-3/4". Those two dimensions cannot both describe the same case, and the plan does not resolve it. The rewrite does not assert a case depth and keeps the plan's own 'size every box to its actual opening' instruction.

DEFECT: the materials note says to rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4" strips for the drawer boxes, while the cut list makes the drawer box sides/fronts/backs 2-1/2" Pine. Two different constructions for the same parts.
GAP: step 3 (original index 2) says to space the front trim 'exactly per the layout'. No layout, drawer-opening height or reveal schedule exists anywhere in the plan.
The six 31-3/4" drawer faces consume both 8 ft 1x4s with about 1/2" of slack across the pair, and the four 32" front trim pieces consume both 8 ft 1x2s — no margin for a mis-cut on either.

step.title: Break down the stock
step.body: Cut four short legs 12-3/4" and eight legs 32" from 2x2; two end panels 12-3/4" x 32" from 3/4" plywood; four slide cleats 12-3/4" and four front trim pieces 32"; six drawer bottoms 16" x 31" from the 1/4" plywood; six drawer faces 31-3/4" from 1x4; and five tabletop boards 36-1/4" from the 1x8s — three off the 10 ft board, two off the 8 ft. Two of the listed counts do not cover their parts: the twelve 2x2 pieces come to 307" against the 288" that three 8 ft 2x2s provide, so buy a fourth; and the drawer box parts plus cleats come to just under 600" of 2-1/2" stock against the 576" six 8 ft 1x3s give, so buy a seventh. The six 31-3/4" faces use both 8 ft 1x4s with roughly 1/2" to spare across the pair, so cut those before anything else touches that stock. Hold the twelve 16" drawer sides and twelve 29-1/2" fronts and backs until the case is standing — every one of them gets cut to the opening you actually end up with, not to these numbers.

ops.body: Each box is two 16" sides running the full depth with the 29-1/2" front and back fitted between them — that is what makes the 16" x 31" plywood bottom right, since 29-1/2" plus 3/4" of side stock at each end is 31". Build all six the same way, pocket-holed and glued, then glue and brad the 1/4" bottom on and use it to pull the box square while the glue sets. Size each box to its own opening rather than to those numbers, less whatever side clearance your slides specify. Square is non-negotiable here: an out-of-square box binds in a Euro slide no matter how carefully the slide is mounted, and no amount of adjustment later will fix it.

baseStepTitles: Join the ends with front and back trim
Install the slides and drawer boxes
Glue up and attach the top

flags: GAP: the tools list has no ripping tool — only a miter saw. Nearly every part is narrower than the picket's 5-1/2" (1", 1-1/4", 1-1/2", 2-1/4", 2-5/8", 3", 3-1/2"), so a table saw or circular saw is required and is not listed.

DERIVED: the 'Mini crate ends' row calls for three 3-1/2" squares but the crate has two ends. With the six 4-1/2" slats on the outside faces, the gap between the ends is exactly 3-1/2", so the third square is the crate floor. Stated as a derivation in the prose, not as an authored fact.
DERIVED: the crate slat gaps (~1/4") are arithmetic — three 1" slats on a 3-1/2" end leave 1/2" across two spaces.
GAP: the business card holder's lean angle is not specified; the rewrite describes the intent without asserting a degree.
One picket is genuinely sufficient — the parts total roughly 157 sq in against the picket's 396 sq in. No shortfall.

ops.body: Cut two front/back pieces 3-1/2" square, two sides 3-1/2" long by 1-5/8" wide, and a bottom 3-1/2" x 2-5/8". The two 3-1/2" front/back pieces run the full width with the 1-5/8" sides fitted between them, which is exactly why the bottom is 2-5/8" — 1-5/8" plus 1/2" of picket at each end. Glue and tack the four walls into a box 3-1/2" x 2-5/8" and 3-1/2" tall, then glue and nail the bottom on underneath so it caps the walls flush. Sand the top rim and break its inside and outside edges; that rim is what your hand touches every time you reach for a pencil.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

ops.body: Cut two front/back pieces 4" long and two sides 3" long, all 1-1/2" wide, plus a 3" square bottom. The 4" front and back run the full length with the 3" sides between them, giving a tray 4" square outside and 3" square inside — which is precisely what the 3" bottom drops into. Glue and pin the four walls first, check both diagonals, then set the bottom flush with the bottom edges. At only 1-1/2" deep a tray shows racking immediately, so get it square before the glue grabs.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

ops.body: Cut all three pieces 4" long: a back 2-1/4" wide, a bottom 1" wide and a front lip 1-1/4" wide. Glue and pin the bottom to the back so the back leans back rather than standing plumb — cards then rest against it and stay readable instead of tipping forward out of the slot. Add the 1-1/4" front lip along the front edge of the bottom, leaving the bottom's own 1/2" thickness as the floor of the slot. At 4" long it takes a standard 3-1/2" business card with room to get a fingertip in alongside.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

ops.body: Cut three 3-1/2" squares and six slats 4-1/2" long by 1" wide. Two of the squares are the crate ends and the slats run across their outside faces, making the crate 4-1/2" long — which leaves exactly 3-1/2" between the ends, so the third square is the floor and drops straight in. Three slats per side on a 3-1/2" end cover 3", leaving two 1/4" gaps to lay out; the gaps are the whole point of a crate, so mark them rather than eyeballing. Sand every piece in all four projects, break the edges, and apply your finish.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 1/2" x 5-1/2" x 6 ft

flags: SHORTFALL (marginal, resolvable from the plan's own stock): four 18" corbel blanks plus three 1/8" kerfs come to 72-3/8", against a 72" 6 ft 4x4 — three blanks fit, the fourth does not. The 10 ft post board leaves 24" after the 96" post, which covers it. Verified with packBoards ([18,18,18,18] @ 72 = 2 boards; [96,18] @ 120 = 1 board).
GAP: the half-lap dimensions are never given. The 1-3/4" depth in the rewrite is half of the 3-1/2" 4x4 thickness — arithmetic, not an authored value.
GAP: where each corbel meets the post, and at what angle, is not specified. The rewrite describes cutting and fitting the joint without asserting a position.
GAP: the post's set depth below grade is not stated anywhere, and neither is the stacking order of the arm, cap boards and trim cap. Both are called out in the prose as builder decisions.
The three 24" cap boards laid side by side make a 16-1/2" x 24" platform (3 x 5-1/2"); that is arithmetic on the cut list, not an authored dimension.
The 2:1 oil-to-spirits first coat is read straight off materials[] (16 oz boiled linseed oil, 8 oz mineral spirits).
The tools list has no tape measure, pencil or square, and assigns the table saw to crosscutting a 10 ft 4x4.

ops.body: Cut the main post 96" from the 10 ft 4x4, trimming past any drying checks at the ends. Four 18" corbel blanks plus their saw kerfs come to a little over 72", so the 6 ft 4x4 yields only three — take the fourth from the 24" left over on the post board. Then cut the 24" mailbox arm from the 2x4, three 24" cap boards from the 2x6s and the 24" trim cap from the 1x6; every one of those is the same 24", so they can all be batched off one stop setting.

A half-lap in 3-1/2" stock means taking 1-3/4" of depth out of each of the two mating pieces, so their faces come flush when the joint closes. Mark the width of the lap off the actual mating part rather than off a tape, set the blade to 1-3/4", and nibble the waste out in repeated passes. Cut one joint and test it before running the rest — cedar is soft, and a lap cut a hair deep will never close tight. Dry-fit every corbel to the post before any glue leaves the bottle; the plan does not fix where on the post each corbel lands, so that position is yours to set now, not after glue-up.

Rout the 1/2" cove along the decorative edges of each corbel in two shallow passes rather than one full-depth cut — cedar tears out badly at the exit if you rush it. Do this while each blank is still a loose piece you can clamp flat to the bench; routing an assembled corbel means routing around the post. Stop the cove short of the half-lap shoulders so the joint still closes on clean, square faces.

Glue the half-laps with Titebond III — it is the waterproof one in that family and the only sensible choice for a joint that lives outdoors for years. Reinforce each lap with #20 biscuits; the plan supplies twelve for four corbels, so that is three per joint. Clamp across the lap with bar clamps, confirm the corbel is square to the post before the glue skins over, and leave it clamped until fully cured rather than until it feels firm.

Screw the 24" 2x4 arm across the top of the post with 3" deck screws, then lay the three 24" 2x6 cap boards side by side to make a 16-1/2" x 24" platform for the mailbox, and finish with the 24" 1x6 trim cap. Predrill every screw — cedar splits readily near an end, and a split at the top of the post is permanent. The plan states neither the stacking order of the cap layers nor how deep the post is set, so dry-assemble the whole top before committing, and set the post depth to your local frost line and to whatever mailbox height your postal service requires.

The plan's 16 oz of boiled linseed oil to 8 oz of mineral spirits is a two-to-one mix; use the thinnest cut for the first coat so it soaks deep into the cedar, then follow with one or two coats closer to straight oil. Wipe off everything that has not been absorbed after fifteen minutes or so — oil left standing on the surface stays tacky and never really cures. Oil-soaked rags generate heat as they dry and are a genuine fire hazard: spread them flat outdoors or drown them in water before they go in a bin.

baseStepTitles: Cut the post and corbel blanks

flags: The plan already opens with a real cut step that names the angle, the measuring convention (long point to short point) and the stop-block technique, so the systemic missing-cut-step gap does not apply here.
The 'Cap the trimmed ends' step calls for short 1x2 end caps that have no cut-list row — expected on a scrap project, but worth noting.
The four divider strips have no specified placement among the twelve field pieces; the plan treats the arrangement as builder's choice, which is defensible for a scrap-bin board.
The 16" field-piece length is explicitly stated in the cut list as an example, so the finished board's overall size is deliberately not fixed. No number in the plan is contradicted by another.

DEFECT / UNRESOLVABLE: the two 79" 2x12 'Headboard/footboard panel boards' are 15-1/2" longer than the 63-1/2" side rail top support, which is the widest cross-member in the bed. Nothing in the plan places a 79" panel board, and the two 56" ones are unexplained too. The rewrite lists them in the cut step and does not assert where they go.

DEFECT: 'Leg wrap boards, ripped' is declared 3-1/2" wide, the same as the unripped 1x4 wraps — but wrapping a 3-1/2" post with 3/4" boards on two opposite faces needs the other two boards at about 5" to close the wrap. The rewrite tells the builder to measure the actual purchased post and rip to suit, which is the only safe reading.
GAP: the footboard has no cut list of its own; only 'the same process, at a shorter height'. No footboard height is given anywhere.
GAP: the mattress-support cleats in the last step, and any slats, appear in neither the cut list nor materials[].

Lumber verified sufficient with packBoards: 1x4 needs 4 (4 listed, ~1/2" total slack), 1x6 needs 2 of 3, 1x3 needs 1 of 1, 2x3 needs 4 of 4, 2x4 needs 3 of 3; the 16 ft 2x12 takes both 79" boards and the 12 ft takes both 56". No shortfall.

step.title: Cut the parts and mark every angle
step.body:
Cut eight 19" leg wraps from 1x4 and eight more 19" wraps from 1x6, ripped to suit the post you actually received. From the rest of the 1x4 cut the headboard main rails at 58-1/8" and 57-3/4" and two vertical fillers at 24"; from the 1x3 cut the 50-7/8" trim rail and three 10-3/4" fillers. Cut two 63-1/2" side rail top supports and two 81-1/2" side rails from 2x3 — one part per 8 ft board — plus two 79" bottom support rails and the 81-3/4" center support from 2x4, the 79" and 56" 2x12 panel boards (both 79s come off the 16 ft board, both 56s off the 12 ft), and the 30" x 57" back panel from 1/4" plywood. The four 8 ft 1x4s cover their parts with only about half an inch of slack in total, so cut the two long headboard rails first.

Write the long point and the short point on every 1/2°-off-square cut the moment it leaves the saw. At half a degree you cannot see which end is which, and the cut list is specific about which parts have parallel ends (the 24" fillers) and which do not (both headboard main rails and the trim rail).

ops.body: Each post takes four 19" wraps: two 1x4s at their full 3-1/2" width on opposite faces, and two ripped from 1x6 wide enough to cover the post plus the 3/4" thickness of those first two boards at each side. Measure your actual post before ripping — the legs are a purchased part and 3-1/2" is the nominal base, not a promise, and the cut list's 3-1/2" figure for the ripped boards would leave the wrap open. Bevel or mitre the top edge of the wrap where it meets the turning above, so the transition reads as one piece rather than a collar. Glue and pin-nail one face at a time, keeping each wrap tight to the post as you go.
materials: Pre-turned bed post legs, ~80" length, 3-1/2" base

ops.body: Pocket-hole a 79" 2x4 bottom support rail between each pair of wrapped legs, low enough that it will end up under the mattress platform rather than in the sleeper's way. Add a 63-1/2" 2x3 top support to the headboard pair the same way; some builds leave it off the footboard end for a more open look and nothing structural depends on it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes and drive 2-1/2" screws into the leg wraps rather than into the turned post — the wrap is what gives you flat, square material to screw to. Check each assembly across the diagonals before the screws go tight.

Cut the 58-1/8" long rail and the 57-3/4" short rail with both ends 1/2° off square and NOT parallel, marking long point and short point on every end as you cut. That 3/8" difference between the two rails is the entire angle — it is what makes the headboard splay slightly instead of sitting dead square, and a rail installed end-for-end looks perfectly fine until nothing else fits. Dry-fit both rails between the leg assemblies before you drill a single pocket hole, then assemble with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws.

Fit the two 24" vertical fillers between the main rails, both ends 1/2° off square and parallel to each other so each filler sits square to the rails it lands on. Measure the actual gap at each position rather than trusting the 24" figure — the angled rails mean the openings are not identical left to right. Pocket-hole them from the back with 1-1/4" screws so no fastener shows on the face, and glue every joint.

The 1x3 trim rail is 50-7/8" with both ends 1/2° off square and not parallel; the three 10-3/4" fillers divide the run below it into four openings. Fit the rail first, then measure each filler position individually before cutting it to length, for the same reason as the last step. Attach everything with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws driven from the back face.

Set the 30" x 57" 1/4" plywood panel into the back of the headboard, centered on the framing so an even margin shows all round. Brad-nail it to the rails and fillers about every 6"; it carries no load, but it closes the openings so the headboard does not read as a bare frame from the bedroom door. If you are using the optional cove moulding, run it around the panel's edge to hide the plywood edge grain.

The footboard repeats the headboard exactly — wrapped legs, a bottom support rail, angled main rails, fillers — but stops short of the headboard's height. The plan gives no separate footboard cut list, so take the width off the assembled headboard and set the height where the mattress and bedding will still show above it. Keep the same 1/2°-off-square rule and the same long-point marking discipline; both ends of the bed have to lean the same way or the side rails will not sit parallel.

Screw a cleat along the inside face of each 81-1/2" side rail with 2-1/2" screws, set at whatever height puts the top of your box spring where you want it. Run the 81-3/4" 2x4 center support down the middle from headboard to footboard and put a leg under its midpoint; at queen width that support earns its keep whether you use a box spring or slats. If you are going without a box spring, lay slats across the cleats and the center support instead. Note that neither the cleats nor any slats appear in the cut list or the materials list, so buy that stock separately.

baseStepTitles: Add the bottom and top rails to the legs
Finish separately, then assemble in the room

flags: DEFECT: the table's center X does not close. Two 30-1/2" half spokes plus the 1-1/2" thickness of the center spoke total 62-1/2" against a 62" center spoke — a 1/2" overrun. The seat framing's equivalent numbers close exactly (46-3/4 + 1-1/2 + 46-3/4 = 95"), which is what proves the table figure is the wrong one. The rewrite says to trim the table half spokes to the real spoke rather than picking a number.

GAP (significant): there are no legs anywhere in this plan. The cut list has no leg part, no step cuts or attaches one, and no table or seat height is given — yet a step says to 'mark all your leg locations'. The rewrite names the gap; it does not invent leg dimensions.

Lumber, verified with packBoards: the framing spokes fit in seven of the ten 8 ft 2x4s, but the 95" seat center spoke leaves about 1" on its 96" board. The 64 tabletop and seat boards need exactly fifteen 8 ft 2x6s — the number listed — with only about 5/16" of slack on the tightest board, so there is effectively no allowance for a mis-cut or for the plan's own advice to fit seat boards by measurement. This is a zero-margin finding, NOT a shortfall claim.

step.title: Cut the framing and the boards
step.body:
Every framing spoke is 2x4 and every top and seat board is 2x6. Cut the table framing first: one 62" center spoke with both ends tapered 30° off square and not parallel to each other, two 30-1/2" half spokes with one end at 30°, and four 29-15/16" point spokes with one end at 30° and the other pointed at 45°. Then the seat framing the same way: one 95" center spoke, two 46-3/4" half spokes and four 46-7/16" point spokes. Seven of the ten 8 ft 2x4s cover all of that, but the 95" seat spoke leaves only about an inch on its board, so pick the straightest, check-free board for it and cut it first.

The tabletop is eight each of six lengths — 4-9/16", 9-1/2", 14-7/16", 19-5/16" and 24-1/4" all with both ends at 22-1/2° off square, and 32-15/16" with both ends at 30° and parallel. The seats are eight at 31-13/16" with 22-1/2° ends and eight at 36-3/4" with parallel 30° ends. Those 64 pieces use all fifteen listed 2x6s with well under an inch of slack on the tightest board, so buy a spare if you intend to follow the plan's advice and fit the seat boards by measurement as you go.

ops.body: On a genuinely flat, level surface, lay the 62" table center spoke down and butt the two 30-1/2" half spokes into its sides to form the cross. Check the arithmetic before you fasten anything: 30-1/2" plus the 1-1/2" thickness of the center spoke plus 30-1/2" is 62-1/2", half an inch longer than the 62" center spoke — the seat framing's equivalent numbers close exactly, so trim the table half spokes to your actual spoke rather than forcing the joint. Join with pocket screws and glue; metal straps or plates work as well, but only on the underside where they cannot foul the tabletop boards. This one joint sets the reference for every other angle on the table, so check it for square and for flat before you go on.

Add the four 29-15/16" point spokes so that all eight arms sit 22-1/2° apart around the center — that is 45° between each point spoke and the cross arm beside it. Each point spoke lands with its 30° end against the side of an existing spoke and its 45° point outward, where two adjacent tabletop boards will meet. Fasten with pocket holes or metal brackets. Measure from the center to each arm tip as you work: if the eight radii are not equal the octagon will not close, and you will discover that when the last tabletop board does not fit.

Flip the table over and mark your leg positions before anything else goes on — and know going in that this plan contains no leg parts at all: no cut-list row, no length, no angle, and no stated table or seat height, so the legs are yours to work out from the three spare 2x4s. Start with the 95" seat center spoke laid across the underside rotated 90° from the tabletop's 62" full-length spoke; that cross-orientation is what gives the underside its real strength, and stacking the two the same way throws it away. Butt the two 46-3/4" half spokes into its sides — 46-3/4 plus 1-1/2 plus 46-3/4 is exactly 95", so this cross does close on its numbers — then add the four 46-7/16" point spokes the same way the tabletop framing went together. If leg room feels tight afterwards, the documented reader variant runs every seat spoke 4" longer and moves the innermost seat board out to the edge.

baseStepTitles: Add the remaining table framing spokes
Lay out and attach the tabletop
